items.original_candidate: First Bethel Baptist Church / Visit by W.E.B. Du Bois
items.new_candidate: Shepherd[']s [Baptist] Church / [observed by] W.E.B. Du Bois [in The Souls of Black Folk, 1903]
items.evidence: Mary Jones explicitly tells the interviewer the elementary school she attended was named 'Shepherd' after the white landowner who donated the church-building and grounds to the Black community in rural Dougherty County: 'We named it after the master that gave us the big church... He gave us that ground and stuff so we named it Shepherd. Elementary school.' She says the school met inside the church building itself ('it was the whole building... first through fifth was in this corner... up until the seventh grade'). This is the same Dougherty County church W.E.B. Du Bois names in The Souls of Black Folk, Chapter VII 'Of the Black Belt' (1903): 'Here is one now, --- Shepherd's, they call it, --- a great whitewashed barn of a thing, perched on stilts of stone...' (Du Bois adds that 'usually the school is held in the church'). The Pass 1 candidate 'First Bethel Baptist Church' has NO external corroboration --- no historical record of a 'First Bethel Baptist Church' in rural Dougherty County (Mt. Zion Baptist (1906) and Shiloh Baptist (1888) are the urban Albany African American Baptist churches; neither matches Mary Jones's description of a rural plantation-donated church she calls 'Shepherd'). The Whisper string 'First, but third' is most plausibly noise/disfluency that does not correspond to a canonical church name; the church Mary Jones is describing is simply called 'Shepherd's Church' in Du Bois's documentation and 'the [Baptist] church' / 'Shepherd' in her own telling. The Du Bois reference is HISTORICALLY CORROBORATED: Du Bois personally visited Dougherty County during his Atlanta University period (1897-1910) and described this exact church in Souls of Black Folk; Mary Jones (born 1933) is recounting the local oral tradition that Du Bois observed/wrote about the church, not claiming he visited during her childhood.
